turns.content: Why do English auctions, Dutch auctions, and sealed-bid auctions produce different results? Doesn’t the highest bidder always win?

“Highest bidder wins” is true mechanically within a given format, but the format changes what people choose to bid, because the strategic problem differs. Different information revelation and different incentives to shade bids imply different equilibrium bids, hence different winners and prices ex ante. A few key distinctions: 1) Information structure: - English (ascending) auctions reveal information through drop-out behavior. Bidders learn something about others’ valuations/signals as the price rises. - Dutch (descending) and first-price sealed-bid reveal no interim information; you choose a stopping price or bid without seeing others’ behavior. 2) Payment rule: - English is essentially a dynamic version of a second-price auction (under private values with independent types): winner pays roughly the second-highest valuation (plus an increment). - First-price sealed-bid and Dutch are “pay your bid” mechanisms: you pay what you bid/stop at. 3) Strategic shading: - In second-price-like formats, truthful bidding is (weakly) dominant under standard private-values assumptions. - In first-price-like formats, you shade below your value to trade off winning probability versus payment. 4) Risk aversion and correlated values: - With risk aversion, bidders shade less in first-price; revenue comparisons can change. - With interdependent/common values, the English auction’s information revelation mitigates the winner’s curse, which changes bidding and can raise expected revenue relative to sealed bid. So, outcomes differ because “who ends up being the highest bidder” is endogenous to the format.
Can you formalize that? Like, write down a model where the equilibrium bids differ across formats.

Sure. Start with the canonical independent private values (IPV) model: - There are n bidders, indexed i = 1,…,n. - Each bidder has a valuation \(v_i\) drawn i.i.d. from distribution F on [0, \(\bar v\)] with density f > 0. - Bidders are risk-neutral and want to maximize expected payoff. - The object is sold to one bidder. First-price sealed-bid auction: Each bidder submits bid \(b_i\). Highest bid wins and pays own bid. If ties, random tie-break. In symmetric Bayes–Nash equilibrium, each bidder uses an increasing bidding function \(b(v)\). A bidder with value v who bids as if they had type x (i.e., bids \(b(x)\)) wins if all others’ values are below x. That probability is \(F(x)^{n-1}\). Expected utility: \[ u(v,x) = (v - b(x)) F(x)^{n-1}. \] In equilibrium, x = v maximizes this. Take derivative w.r.t. x and set to zero at x=v: \[ \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -(b'(x))F(x)^{n-1} + (v - b(x))(n-1)F(x)^{n-2}f(x). \] At x=v: \[ -b'(v)F(v)^{n-1} + (v - b(v))(n-1)F(v)^{n-2}f(v) = 0. \] Rearrange: \[ b'(v) = (v - b(v)) \frac{(n-1) f(v)}{F(v)}. \] This is a first-order linear ODE. Solve with boundary condition \(b(0)=0\) (a type with zero value bids zero). The solution: \[ b(v) = v - \int_{0}^{v} \left(\frac{F(t)}{F(v)}\right)^{n-1} dt. \] For example, if F is uniform on [0,1], then \(F(t)=t\), and: \[ b(v) = v - \int_0^v \left(\frac{t}{v}\right)^{n-1} dt = v - v \int_0^1 y^{n-1} dy = v - v\cdot \frac{1}{n} = \frac{n-1}{n}v. \] Second-price sealed-bid: Bidders submit bids; highest bid wins; pays second-highest bid. Under IPV and risk neutrality, bidding \(b(v)=v\) is weakly dominant: your bid only affects whether you win, not the price you pay conditional on winning (it’s determined by others). So equilibrium bids differ: first-price shades, second-price is truthful. English ascending: In IPV with standard assumptions and small increments, the English auction is strategically equivalent to second-price: staying in until price reaches your value is a weakly dominant strategy. Outcome: same allocation and (in expectation) same revenue as second-price. Dutch descending: In IPV, Dutch is strategically equivalent to first-price: choosing a stopping price is like submitting a bid; you win if you stop before others. So equilibrium shading matches first-price. That’s the formal sense in which formats differ: their equilibrium bid functions differ even though each format awards to “highest bid.”
If English and second-price are equivalent, why do people say English auctions can yield higher prices sometimes?

Because the equivalence relies on IPV and certain idealizations. When you relax them, English can produce higher expected revenue. Two big departures: 1) Interdependent values / common value components: Suppose each bidder observes a signal \(s_i\) about an unknown common component V. Your “value” depends on V, which others’ signals inform. In sealed-bid, you must infer V from your own signal; if you win, that’s bad news (winner’s curse), so you shade aggressively. In an English auction, as others drop out you infer their signals are low/high; that information reduces uncertainty and mitigates the winner’s curse, so bidders stay in longer. This can raise the final price. 2) Affiliation / correlated signals: If signals are affiliated (a form of positive dependence), the Milgrom–Weber linkage principle shows that more informative mechanisms (like English) can yield higher expected revenue than less informative ones, because information revelation increases bidders’ willingness to pay. So “English ≈ second-price” is a special case; with common values and affiliated signals, English can outperform sealed-bid in expected revenue.
Can you sketch a simple common-value example where sealed bids suffer from the winner’s curse?

Consider a pure common-value model: - True value \(V\) is random. - Each bidder i observes signal \(s_i = V + \epsilon_i\), where \(\epsilon_i\) are i.i.d. noise with mean 0. - Everyone values the object at V (same for all). In a first-price sealed bid, you might think “bid E[V|s_i]”. But conditional on winning, your signal tends to be the highest, which means you likely overestimated V. The correct object to bid against is \(E[V \mid s_i, \text{win}]\), which is less than \(E[V \mid s_i]\). That difference is the winner’s curse adjustment. Formally, in symmetric equilibrium, a bidder with signal s chooses bid b(s). Winning means your signal is the maximum among n signals. So: \[ E[V \mid s_i = s, \text{win}] = E[V \mid s_i=s, s_i = \max_j s_j]. \] This conditional expectation is below \(E[V \mid s_i=s]\) because “being the max” pushes the noise term positive on average, so you subtract an expected positive bias. In an English auction, as the price rises, some bidders drop out at prices reflecting their signals. Observing dropouts gives you information about their signals, which refines your estimate of V and reduces the conditional bias from being the max. You therefore bid closer to the refined estimate, leading to higher expected final prices relative to sealed bids.
I want a more mathematical statement of the linkage principle. What exactly does it say?

In broad terms (Milgrom–Weber 1982), in a symmetric interdependent-values environment with affiliated signals, auction formats that reveal more information about signals generate higher expected revenue (and higher expected seller surplus), holding other things equal. A more explicit version: - There are n bidders with signals \(s_i\) drawn from a joint distribution with affiliation (log-supermodularity). - Each bidder’s value \(v_i\) can depend on all signals, often written \(v_i = v(s_i, s_{-i})\) satisfying monotonicity conditions. - Consider standard auction formats with symmetric equilibrium. Then among a class of “standard” mechanisms, the expected revenue ranking typically is: English (ascending) ≥ Second-price sealed-bid ≥ First-price sealed-bid ≈ Dutch, with weak inequalities, and strict under conditions where information revelation matters. The intuition is that more information increases the expected value conditional on winning (reduces winner’s curse), raising bids. Technically, the proof uses affiliation to show that conditional expectations like \(E[v_i \mid s_i, \text{event about others’ signals}]\) are increasing in that event, and that the price in more revealing auctions is more tightly linked to others’ signals, which increases expected payments.
You said first-price and Dutch are equivalent in IPV. Is that exact or only approximate?

Under the standard IPV assumptions, it’s exact in terms of equilibrium outcomes. A Dutch auction is a descending clock: price starts high and falls until someone accepts. Each bidder chooses a stopping price p at which they will accept. If everyone uses a monotone strategy p(v), then the event “i wins” is “i’s stopping price is the highest among bidders,” which is equivalent to “i’s bid is the highest” in a first-price sealed-bid. The payment is the stopping price—i.e., your own chosen number—same as first-price. So the strategic form differs (dynamic vs sealed), but the equilibrium mapping is isomorphic: the same bid function b(v) solves both. Approximation enters only if you add discrete time/price ticks, jump bids, or behavioral issues; then the equivalence can break.
In first-price auctions, can we derive expected revenue and compare it to second-price under IPV?

Yes, and under IPV with risk neutrality and independent types, you get the revenue equivalence theorem: standard auctions that allocate efficiently (highest value wins) and give zero expected surplus to the lowest type yield the same expected revenue. For a concrete calculation, take n bidders, values i.i.d. Uniform[0,1]. Second-price: Truthful bidding implies winner is highest value \(V_{(n)}\) and pays second-highest \(V_{(n-1)}\). Expected revenue is \(E[V_{(n-1)}]\). For Uniform[0,1], order statistic expectation: \[ E[V_{(k)}] = \frac{k}{n+1}. \] So \(E[V_{(n-1)}] = \frac{n-1}{n+1}\). First-price: Equilibrium bid is \(b(v)=\frac{n-1}{n}v\). Winner’s value is \(V_{(n)}\). Payment is \(b(V_{(n)}) = \frac{n-1}{n} V_{(n)}\). Expected revenue: \[ E\left[\frac{n-1}{n}V_{(n)}\right] = \frac{n-1}{n} \cdot E[V_{(n)}] = \frac{n-1}{n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} = \frac{n-1}{n+1}. \] Same as second-price. That’s a direct demonstration of revenue equivalence in this case.
Revenue equivalence always holds? What breaks it?

It holds under a specific set of conditions; it’s fragile outside them. Key assumptions: 1) Independent private values (or more generally, symmetric independent types with private information). 2) Risk neutrality. 3) Allocation rule: object goes to highest “type” (efficient allocation). 4) Lowest type expects zero surplus (individual rationality normalization). 5) Bids/strategies are in symmetric equilibrium and payments satisfy certain regularity. What breaks it: - Risk aversion: first-price can yield higher expected revenue than second-price because risk-averse bidders shade less to reduce win-probability risk. - Interdependent/common values or affiliated signals: information revelation changes conditional values; English can dominate. - Asymmetries (different F’s, different risk attitudes): revenue rankings can change. - Reserve prices and entry: optimal auction design changes, and formats can differ in entry incentives. - Collusion or bidder behavior: dynamic auctions may facilitate collusion or signaling, affecting revenue. - Budget constraints: can bind differently across formats. - Multi-unit or combinatorial settings: the theorem is for single-item with certain properties; multi-unit introduces new strategic effects.
Let’s go deeper into risk aversion. How does the first-price equilibrium change?

Suppose utility is \(u(w)\) with u increasing and concave; bidder with value v who pays p and wins gets utility \(u(v-p)\); losing yields \(u(0)\) (normalize). In a first-price auction with symmetric increasing bid function b(v), if a type v deviates to bid as type x, win probability is \(F(x)^{n-1}\). Expected utility: \[ U(v,x) = F(x)^{n-1} u(v - b(x)) + (1 - F(x)^{n-1}) u(0). \] In equilibrium x=v maximizes. Differentiate at x=v: \[ 0 = \frac{\partial U}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=v} = (n-1)F(v)^{n-2}f(v)\left[u(v-b(v)) - u(0)\right] - F(v)^{n-1} u'(v-b(v)) b'(v). \] Rearrange: \[ b'(v) = \frac{(n-1) f(v)}{F(v)} \cdot \frac{u(v-b(v)) - u(0)}{u'(v-b(v))}. \] Compare to risk-neutral case where u(z)=z and the fraction becomes v-b(v). With concavity, \(\frac{u(z)-u(0)}{u'(z)} \le z\) (by concavity, since u lies below its tangent line), implying b'(v) is smaller or larger? Careful: If \(\frac{u(z)-u(0)}{u'(z)} \le z\), then the RHS is ≤ \(\frac{(n-1)f}{F}(v-b(v))\). But b itself changes; the typical result is that for risk-averse bidders, equilibrium bids are higher (less shading) than risk-neutral. Intuitively, they’re willing to pay more to increase winning probability and reduce uncertainty. One can show monotone comparative statics: more risk aversion shifts b(v) upward pointwise (under regularity). Consequently, first-price expected revenue increases relative to second-price, where truthful bidding remains weakly dominant in IPV even with risk aversion? Actually, in second-price, with private values and independent types, truthful bidding is still weakly dominant regardless of risk preferences because payment doesn’t depend on your bid when you win, only the win/loss threshold does. So second-price bids remain at value. Therefore, risk aversion affects first-price but not second-price, breaking revenue equivalence.
Wait—why does risk aversion not change second-price bidding? You still face uncertainty about winning.

In second-price, your bid determines only the set of prices at which you win: you win if your bid exceeds the highest other bid. But conditional on winning, the price you pay is the highest other bid, independent of your own bid (as long as you still win). So: - If you bid below your value, you may lose in cases where the price you would have paid is below your value—foregoing positive utility. - If you bid above your value, you may win in cases where the price exceeds your value—incurring negative utility. This argument does not use linear utility; it uses monotonicity: you prefer winning at prices below value and prefer losing at prices above value. With risk aversion, that preference ordering remains. Hence bidding your true value is a weakly dominant strategy. More formally: For any fixed profile of others’ bids, the outcome as a function of your bid is: lose for bids below some threshold t (the highest other bid), win and pay t for bids above t. If t < v, you strictly prefer to win; if t > v, you strictly prefer to lose; if t=v, indifferent. Thus bidding v ensures you win iff t < v, which is optimal pointwise. No expected utility calculation needed.
Ok. Now, in first-price, you derived an ODE under risk neutrality. Can you derive the expected bidder surplus too?

Under IPV, risk neutrality, symmetric equilibrium b(v). The interim expected surplus for a bidder of type v is: \[ U(v) = (v - b(v)) F(v)^{n-1}. \] Ex ante expected surplus (before knowing v) is \(E[U(V)]\) where V ~ F. For Uniform[0,1] with b(v)=((n-1)/n)v: Interim surplus: \[ U(v) = \left(v - \frac{n-1}{n}v\right) v^{n-1} = \frac{1}{n} v^n. \] Ex ante: \[ E[U(V)] = \int_0^1 \frac{1}{n} v^n dv = \frac{1}{n}\cdot \frac{1}{n+1} = \frac{1}{n(n+1)}. \] That’s per bidder. Total bidder surplus is n times that: \[ \frac{1}{n+1}. \] Seller expected revenue we computed as \((n-1)/(n+1)\). Total expected social surplus is expected highest value \(E[V_{(n)}]=n/(n+1)\), and indeed: \[ \text{Revenue} + \text{Total bidder surplus} = \frac{n-1}{n+1} + \frac{1}{n+1} = \frac{n}{n+1}. \]
Good. Now, can you explain why the equilibrium in first-price is monotone? Is that assumed or derived?

In most treatments it’s derived (or at least justified) under regularity conditions. Key idea: single-crossing / incentive compatibility implies monotonicity. If higher valuations didn’t bid at least as high as lower valuations, there would be profitable deviations. Sketch: Assume a symmetric equilibrium strategy b(v). Suppose there exist \(v_1 < v_2\) but \(b(v_1) > b(v_2)\). Consider type \(v_2\). By bidding \(b(v_1)\) instead of \(b(v_2)\), type \(v_2\) weakly increases winning probability (since higher bid) while paying more conditional on winning. But because \(v_2\) has higher value, the marginal cost of paying more is lower (in terms of surplus loss) than for \(v_1\). One can formalize via the fact that expected payoff as a function of bid has increasing differences in (v, bid). This yields that best responses are nondecreasing in v. More formally, in IPV risk-neutral first-price, payoff from bidding b when others use increasing strategy is: \[ \pi(v,b) = (v-b) G(b) \] where \(G(b)\) is probability of winning with bid b (increasing in b). This payoff has increasing differences in (v, b): for \(v'>v\), \[ \pi(v',b) - \pi(v,b) = (v'-v)G(b), \] which is increasing in b because G(b) increases in b. By Topkis’ theorem (monotone comparative statics), the set of optimal bids is nondecreasing in v. Under uniqueness, b(v) is increasing. So monotonicity is a consequence of the structure.
How do reserves change things? Like optimal reserve price in Myerson?

Myerson (1981) solves optimal auction design under IPV with independent types and risk-neutral bidders. The key object is the virtual value: \[ \phi(v) = v - \frac{1-F(v)}{f(v)}. \] Optimal auction allocates the good to the bidder with the highest nonnegative virtual value (subject to incentive compatibility). In the symmetric regular case (where \(\phi(v)\) is increasing), this is equivalent to using a reserve price r defined by \(\phi(r)=0\). Then run a standard auction (e.g., second-price) with reserve r: allocate to highest bidder if their bid ≥ r; charge max(reserve, second-highest bid). For Uniform[0,1], \(F(v)=v\), \(f(v)=1\), so: \[ \phi(v) = v - (1-v) = 2v-1. \] Set \(\phi(r)=0\) → r=1/2. So optimal reserve is 0.5. Reserves break simple revenue equivalence comparisons if the compared auctions use different reserves or induce different entry. But if you take two auction formats that implement the same allocation rule with the same reserve and satisfy the theorem’s conditions, revenue equivalence can still apply.
In first-price with reserve, is the equilibrium still solvable?

Yes, though the boundary condition changes. Suppose seller sets a reserve price r: bids below r are rejected; if all bids below r, no sale. In first-price, in symmetric equilibrium, types below some cutoff may bid below r and effectively not win; types above bid above r. Typically, the lowest participating type is v=r (in symmetric equilibrium with monotone bidding), and the boundary condition becomes \(b(r)=r\) (you must bid at least r to win). For v<r, optimal is to bid below r (or not participate), yielding zero chance to win and zero payoff. For v ≥ r, the ODE becomes the same as before: \[ b'(v) = (v - b(v)) \frac{(n-1) f(v)}{F(v)} \] but now solved on [r, \(\bar v\)] with boundary condition b(r)=r. The solution: \[ b(v) = v - \int_{r}^{v} \left(\frac{F(t)}{F(v)}\right)^{n-1} dt \cdot \left(\frac{F(r)}{F(v)}\right)^{-(n-1)}? \] Let me write it carefully. Solve the linear ODE: \[ b'(v) + b(v)\frac{(n-1)f(v)}{F(v)} = v \frac{(n-1)f(v)}{F(v)}. \] Use integrating factor \(M(v)=F(v)^{n-1}\). Then: \[ \frac{d}{dv}[b(v)F(v)^{n-1}] = v (n-1) f(v) F(v)^{n-2}. \] Integrate from r to v: \[ b(v)F(v)^{n-1} - b(r)F(r)^{n-1} = \int_r^v t (n-1) f(t) F(t)^{n-2} dt. \] Thus: \[ b(v) = \frac{b(r)F(r)^{n-1} + \int_r^v t (n-1) f(t) F(t)^{n-2} dt}{F(v)^{n-1}}. \] And b(r)=r. That gives a closed-form expression given F.
Nice. How does this connect to business applications? Like ad auctions?

Many ad auctions are multi-unit, repeated, and have quality adjustments, but auction theory concepts carry over. For search ads, a common stylized model is a generalized second-price (GSP) auction: - Multiple slots with click-through rates \(\alpha_1 \ge \alpha_2 \ge \dots\). - Each advertiser i has value per click \(v_i\) and quality score \(q_i\). - Ranking often by \(b_i q_i\). - Payment per click is the minimum needed to keep your position: roughly \(\frac{b_{i+1} q_{i+1}}{q_i}\). GSP is not a truthful mechanism generally, unlike VCG (Vickrey–Clarke–Groves). But in many models there exist equilibria (e.g., symmetric Nash equilibria) that approximate the VCG outcome under certain assumptions. Auction format matters: first-price vs second-price. Notably, many ad exchanges have shifted toward first-price auctions recently, changing bidder behavior: shading algorithms, bid optimization, and different revenue dynamics. The reason is exactly what we discussed: in first-price, bidders shade below value; in second-price, they can bid closer to value but then deal with reserve prices and platform-specific modifications (like soft floors) that can undermine truthfulness and change incentives.
But GSP isn’t the same as second-price. Can you formalize why it’s not truthful?

In single-item second-price, your payment if you win is independent of your bid (it depends on others). In GSP with multiple slots, your bid affects not only whether you win but which slot you get, and the price you pay depends on the next bidder below you, but your bid affects who is below you and which slot you occupy. This creates strategic interdependence. A simple counterexample: Two slots with click-through rates \(\alpha_1 > \alpha_2 > 0\). Three bidders with values per click \(v_1, v_2, v_3\). Suppose ranking is by bids (ignore quality). If bidder 1 is highest, they get slot 1 and pay \(b_2\) per click; bidder 2 gets slot 2 and pays \(b_3\). Bidder 1’s utility if in slot 1: \(\alpha_1 (v_1 - b_2)\). If bidder 1 shades and ends up in slot 2: \(\alpha_2 (v_1 - b_3)\). Depending on parameters, bidder 1 may prefer slot 2 at a lower price even if their value is highest. Then bidding truthfully to maximize chance of slot 1 is not necessarily optimal. The “dominant strategy” property breaks because there are multiple outcomes with different prices and quantities (click volume). Truthfulness requires the mechanism to be VCG-like; GSP is not VCG though it has equilibria that can correspond to VCG payments in certain settings (the “VCG-like equilibrium”).
Let’s go back. You mentioned affiliation. What is affiliation exactly? I want the definition.

Affiliation is a strong form of positive dependence among random variables. For signals \(S_1,\dots,S_n\) with joint density \(f(s)\) on some lattice (often \(\mathbb{R}^n\)), they are affiliated if for all \(s, s'\), \[ f(s \vee s') f(s \wedge s') \ge f(s) f(s'), \] where \((s \vee s')\) is the componentwise maximum and \((s \wedge s')\) is the componentwise minimum. Equivalently, the joint density is log-supermodular: \(\log f(s)\) is supermodular. Intuitively, high realizations of some components make high realizations of others more likely. Affiliation implies monotone likelihood ratio properties and positive regression dependence, which are used in auction revenue comparisons: observing that others have high signals increases your posterior about the common component/value.
In the private-values IPV model, do we need independence? What if values are correlated but still private?

Correlated private values can break some classic results, especially revenue equivalence and the equivalence between English and second-price in terms of bidding behavior (though allocation may still be efficient). If values are correlated, your information about your own value also conveys information about others’ values. In a second-price auction, truthful bidding can fail to be dominant because your bid can affect the price you pay indirectly through informational effects? Actually, in a standard sealed-bid second-price with private values, your bid still doesn’t affect the price conditional on winning given others’ bids; so the pointwise dominance logic still seems to go through. But correlation can matter because “your value” might not be independent of others, yet the dominance argument is purely ex post given others’ bids, so truthful bidding remains weakly dominant as long as your value is independent of your bid and you have quasi-linear utility. So second-price truthfulness survives correlation in private values. Where correlation bites more is revenue equivalence and optimal design: with correlated types, the seller can sometimes extract more revenue using mechanisms that condition payments on others’ reports (Cremer–McLean). Also, in English auctions, dropout information may change beliefs about others’ values, affecting strategies and potentially revenue. So: correlation doesn’t kill second-price truth-telling, but it changes comparative statics, revenue, and optimal mechanisms.
Cremer–McLean sounds extreme. What’s the idea?

In environments with sufficiently rich correlation among bidders’ signals/types, a seller can design a mechanism that extracts the entire expected surplus (leaving bidders zero expected utility) while still satisfying incentive compatibility and participation constraints (in expectation). The mechanism uses side payments contingent on others’ reported types to “insure” away information rents. Because types are correlated, a bidder’s report changes the distribution of others’ reports. The seller can set transfers so that truthful reporting maximizes expected utility but expected utility is driven to zero (or arbitrarily close), effectively capturing surplus. Technically, it requires a “full rank” condition on the conditional distributions of others’ types given a bidder’s type. It’s often viewed as unrealistic because it can imply large transfers, requires precise knowledge of distributions, and can violate limited liability.
Interesting. Now, can we discuss efficiency? Which auction format is efficient?

Under IPV with increasing strategies, standard auctions (first-price, second-price, English, Dutch) are efficient: the bidder with the highest value wins. This follows because equilibrium bidding functions are increasing in value, so highest value corresponds to highest bid/stopping price. With reserves, efficiency is reduced (intentionally) because some positive-value trades are blocked to increase revenue. With interdependent values, efficiency depends on mechanism design; standard formats may not allocate to the highest ex post value because values depend on signals and information is incomplete. But in symmetric interdependent models with monotone strategies, English auctions often remain efficient in terms of allocating to the highest signal (which may correspond to highest expected value), though ex post realized value might differ. With risk aversion, allocation remains efficient in the single-item case because monotonicity still holds: higher values bid higher. So efficiency is robust in the canonical model; revenue and bidding differ more than efficiency.
I want to see the derivation of the first-price bid function in general again, but maybe using the envelope theorem.

Using the envelope theorem is elegant. Let b(v) be symmetric increasing equilibrium. Define interim utility: \[ U(v) = (v - b(v)) F(v)^{n-1}. \] In incentive compatible direct mechanisms, envelope theorem gives \(U'(v) = q(v)\), where q(v) is allocation probability. In the first-price auction equilibrium, allocation probability for type v is \(q(v)=F(v)^{n-1}\) because you win iff your value is highest (given monotone bids). So: \[ U'(v) = F(v)^{n-1}. \] Integrate from 0 to v with boundary U(0)=0: \[ U(v) = \int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt. \] But also U(v) = (v - b(v))F(v)^{n-1}. Therefore: \[ (v - b(v))F(v)^{n-1} = \int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt. \] Solve for b(v): \[ b(v) = v - \frac{\int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt}{F(v)^{n-1}}. \] This matches the earlier expression (equivalent after algebra). It’s a nice demonstration that the equilibrium payments are pinned down by allocation probabilities plus boundary conditions (the core of revenue equivalence).
Great. Now, can you explain the difference between Bayes–Nash and dominant strategy equilibrium in this context?

Dominant strategy equilibrium (DSE): each bidder has a strategy that is optimal regardless of others’ strategies/types. In single-item second-price and English (under IPV), bidding truthfully (or staying until value) is a dominant strategy: you don’t need beliefs about others. Bayes–Nash equilibrium (BNE): each bidder’s strategy is optimal given beliefs about others’ types and given others’ strategies, maximizing expected utility. First-price and Dutch generally have BNE but not DSE: your optimal bid depends on the distribution of others’ valuations and the number of bidders. So the “format matters” partly because some formats yield DSE (simpler, robust) and others yield BNE (sensitive to beliefs, risk preferences, etc.).
What about all-pay auctions? Where everyone pays their bid.

All-pay auctions: each bidder pays their bid regardless of winning; highest bid wins. They model contests (lobbying, R&D races, rent-seeking). In IPV with risk-neutral bidders, symmetric equilibrium can be derived similarly. Let b(v) be equilibrium bid. If type v bids as if type x, expected payoff: \[ u(v,x) = v \cdot F(x)^{n-1} - b(x). \] Because you pay b(x) regardless of winning. First-order condition at x=v: \[ \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = v (n-1)F(x)^{n-2}f(x) - b'(x). \] At x=v: \[ b'(v) = v (n-1)F(v)^{n-2}f(v). \] Integrate with b(0)=0: \[ b(v) = \int_0^v t (n-1)F(t)^{n-2}f(t)\, dt. \] For Uniform[0,1], f=1, F=t: \[ b(v) = \int_0^v t (n-1) t^{n-2} dt = (n-1)\int_0^v t^{n-1} dt = (n-1)\frac{v^n}{n} = \frac{n-1}{n}v^n. \] Expected revenue in all-pay equals expected highest value? Not exactly; but expected total payments can be computed and, under revenue equivalence conditions (allocation to highest value and zero utility for lowest type), the seller’s expected revenue matches that of other standard auctions. Indeed, all-pay also satisfies revenue equivalence in IPV risk-neutral case.
How can all-pay satisfy revenue equivalence? The payment structure is so different.

Revenue equivalence is about expected revenue, not payment incidence. In all-pay, losing bidders pay too, but equilibrium bids are correspondingly lower in a way that keeps expected total payments equal to the expected second-highest value (in the symmetric IPV case). One way to see it: revenue equivalence theorem says expected payment of a type v is pinned down by allocation probability q(v) and utility normalization. Since all these standard auctions allocate to the highest value with the same q(v)=F(v)^{n-1}, the interim expected payment function must be the same across them (up to constants). All-pay just implements that expected payment through unconditional payments rather than conditional on winning. You can verify in Uniform[0,1], n bidders: - Second-price expected revenue: (n-1)/(n+1). - All-pay expected total payment: n * E[b(V)] = n * E[(n-1)/n V^n] = (n-1) E[V^n] = (n-1)/(n+1). Same number.
Let’s talk about entry. How does auction format affect entry decisions?

With endogenous entry, formats can differ because bidders’ expected profits (net of entry costs) differ under different information structures and risk. Simple model: - Potential bidders decide to enter at cost c. - After entering, they learn valuation v and bid in the auction. - Entry equilibrium: expected profit from entering equals c (in mixed or marginal entrant sense). Under IPV and revenue equivalence with fixed n, expected bidder surplus is the same across standard auctions. But with entry, the number of entrants n becomes endogenous; small differences in expected surplus can change n. If revenue equivalence holds exactly and entry is symmetric, the induced entry might be similar. But deviations (risk aversion, common values, etc.) can change expected surplus and thus entry. Also, auction format can affect the cost of participation (e.g., time spent in an English auction), which changes entry. Dynamic auctions may deter entry if they require monitoring, potentially reducing competition and revenue. So, entry is a key channel by which format matters in practice.
I’m still curious: in English auctions with private values, is “stay until your value” strictly dominant?

It’s weakly dominant, not strictly. If you have value v, strategy “drop out when price reaches v” ensures: - You never win at a price above v (avoids negative surplus). - You win whenever the price is below v and others drop out earlier, yielding nonnegative surplus. But there can be histories where multiple strategies yield the same payoff (e.g., if you would lose anyway because someone else has higher value). In those cases, deviating doesn’t change outcome, hence dominance is weak. Strict dominance would require that any deviation yields strictly lower payoff in all contingencies, which is not true.
Can you connect English auction to the second-price more formally? Like equivalence in outcome distribution.

Under IPV with continuous values and no bid increment issues, if everyone follows the weakly dominant strategy (stay until v), then: - The winner is the bidder with highest valuation \(V_{(n)}\). - The price at which the second-highest bidder drops out is \(V_{(n-1)}\). - The final price paid by the winner is approximately \(V_{(n-1)}\) (plus minimal increment). Thus the distribution of allocation and payments matches the second-price auction: winner pays the second-highest valuation. If increments are discrete of size Δ, the winner pays \(V_{(n-1)} + \Delta\) (or the smallest grid price above), creating a small difference.
What about tie probabilities? In continuous models, ties have probability zero, but in practice they matter.

In continuous-type models, ties are measure zero so equilibrium characterization ignores them. With discrete valuations or discrete bid grids, ties occur with positive probability and can affect equilibrium. In first-price with discrete bids, equilibrium may involve mixed strategies or multiple equilibria due to indifference across adjacent bids. Tie-breaking rules matter (random vs priority). Revenue equivalence can fail discretely. In second-price, ties can make truthful bidding less clean because tie-breaking can depend on bids; still, bidding your value is often weakly dominant but you may have incentives to slightly overbid to improve tie-breaking if ties are common. Many platforms specify tie-breaking independent of bid to preserve incentive properties, or use randomization. So tie probability is a practical implementation detail that can change incentives.
I’d like to see a discrete example where first-price equilibrium involves mixing.

Consider two bidders (n=2). Values are discrete: v ∈ {0,1,2} with equal probability 1/3, independent. Allowed bids are {0,1,2}. First-price auction, highest bid wins, ties split evenly. We can attempt pure strategies b(v). Monotonicity suggests b(0)=0, b(2)=2 maybe, but b(1) is tricky: - If type 1 bids 1, they win against type 0, tie against type 1, lose against type 2. - If type 1 bids 2, they may win more often but pay 2, yielding negative payoff when winning (since v=1). So bidding 2 is bad. - If type 1 bids 0, they lose too much. So likely b(1)=1 is plausible. But check type 2: if b(2)=2, then against type 1 they win and pay 2, surplus 0, whereas if they bid 1 they tie with type 1 and pay 1 when win, surplus 1. That might be better. So type 2 may want to shade to 1. But then type 1 might want to adjust. Because of discreteness, you can get situations where type 2 mixes between 1 and 2 to keep type 1 indifferent, etc. Solving fully involves writing indifference conditions for mixed strategies. The general point: discrete bids create nonconvexities; pure-strategy monotone equilibrium may not exist, and mixed equilibria appear. If you want, we can solve a fully specified discrete case with mixing probabilities, but it gets algebraic; the qualitative takeaway is that discretization complicates equilibrium and can break clean equivalences.
Let’s do it. Solve a small discrete case completely.

Let’s pick an even simpler discrete environment that yields a clean mixed equilibrium. Two bidders. Values v ∈ {1,2} with equal probability 1/2. Bids allowed: {1,2}. First-price. Tie split 1/2. Let strategies be: - Type 1 must bid 1 (bidding 2 gives negative surplus if wins). - Type 2 may mix: bid 1 with probability p, bid 2 with probability 1-p. Compute payoffs. If you are type 2 and bid 2: - If opponent is type 1 (prob 1/2), opponent bids 1, you win, pay 2, surplus 0. - If opponent is type 2 (prob 1/2), opponent bids 2 with prob 1-p and 1 with prob p. - If opponent bids 2: tie, win prob 1/2, surplus 0. - If opponent bids 1: you win, surplus 0. So bidding 2 yields expected payoff 0. If type 2 bids 1: - If opponent is type 1 (prob 1/2): tie at 1? Actually type 1 bids 1, so tie; win prob 1/2; if win pay 1 surplus 1. Expected payoff = (1/2)*1 = 1/2. - If opponent is type 2 (prob 1/2): - opponent bids 2 with prob 1-p: you lose, payoff 0. - opponent bids 1 with prob p: tie; win prob 1/2; surplus 1. Expected payoff = p*(1/2)*1 = p/2. So total expected payoff from bidding 1 is: \[ \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{p}{2} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{p}{4} = \frac{1+p}{4}. \] Thus type 2 strictly prefers bidding 1 for any p≥0, since (1+p)/4 > 0. Therefore no mixing: type 2 bids 1 always. But then type 1 is indifferent? Type 1 bidding 1 yields expected payoff: - Against type 1 (prob 1/2): tie, win prob 1/2, surplus 0 → 0. - Against type 2 (prob 1/2): type 2 bids 1, tie, win prob 1/2, surplus 0 → 0. So type 1 payoff 0. Deviating to 2 gives negative expected payoff when winning (value 1, pay 2), so not. So equilibrium: both types bid 1 always. That’s degenerate and inefficient in bids (but allocation still random among ties). This illustrates a problem: too coarse bidding. Let’s expand bid set to {1, 1.5, 2} and values {1,2}. Now type 1 might bid 1, type 2 might mix between 1.5 and 2, etc. But still, type 2 paying 2 yields zero surplus, so they’d prefer 1.5 if it still wins often. With only two types, mixing may still not arise cleanly. A better discrete mixing example is all-pay or wars of attrition. For first-price, mixing arises with more types and restricted bids. Let’s do: values v ∈ {1,2,3} equally likely, bids {1,2,3}. Two bidders. Conjecture: type 1 bids 1, type 3 bids 2 or 3, type 2 mixes. We can solve systematically by allowing mixing for types 2 and 3. Let: - Type 1 bids 1. - Type 2 bids 1 with prob a, bids 2 with prob 1-a. - Type 3 bids 2 with prob b, bids 3 with prob 1-b. (Type 3 never bids 1 in plausible equilibrium; we’ll verify.) Compute expected payoffs: First compute opponent bid distribution: Opponent is type 1 w.p. 1/3 → bid 1. Opponent is type 2 w.p. 1/3 → bid 1 w.p. a, bid 2 w.p. 1-a. Opponent is type 3 w.p. 1/3 → bid 2 w.p. b, bid 3 w.p. 1-b. So: P(opponent bids 1) = 1/3 + (1/3)a = (1+a)/3. P(opponent bids 2) = (1/3)(1-a) + (1/3)b = (1-a+b)/3. P(opponent bids 3) = (1/3)(1-b) = (1-b)/3. Now type 2’s expected payoff if bidding 1: - Win if opponent bids <1: impossible. Tie if opponent bids 1: win prob 1/2, surplus (2-1)=1. - Lose if opponent bids 2 or 3. So: \[ \pi_2(1) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P(\text{opp bids }1)\cdot 1 = \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{1+a}{3} = \frac{1+a}{6}. \] Type 2’s payoff if bidding 2: - Win if opponent bids 1: surplus (2-2)=0. - Tie if opponent bids 2: win prob 1/2, surplus 0. - Lose if opponent bids 3. So payoff is 0. Thus type 2 strictly prefers bidding 1 as long as (1+a)/6 > 0, which it is. So type 2 never bids 2 in equilibrium. That collapses again. This is showing a general issue: with first-price and discrete bids equal to values, higher bids can eliminate surplus entirely, so types prefer low bids. To get meaningful mixing, we need bid grid finer than values so that higher types can outbid lower types without giving up all surplus. Let’s instead do: values {1,2,3} and bids {0,1,2}. Two bidders. Now type 3 can bid 2 and still have surplus 1, etc. Type 1 probably bids 0 or 1, type 2 bids 1 or 2, etc. This can generate mixing. Set: - Values v ∈ {1,2,3}, each with prob 1/3. - Allowed bids: {0,1,2}. - First-price, tie split. We seek symmetric equilibrium with possible mixing for v=2 and v=3. Let: - Type 1 bids 0 with prob x, 1 with prob 1-x. (Type 1 won’t bid 2.) - Type 2 bids 1 with prob y, 2 with prob 1-y. (Type 2 won’t bid 0 typically.) - Type 3 bids 2 always (we’ll check). Compute opponent bid distribution: P(opp bid 0) = (1/3)x. P(opp bid 1) = (1/3)(1-x) + (1/3)y = (1 - x + y)/3. P(opp bid 2) = (1/3)(1-y) + (1/3)*1 = (2 - y)/3. Now best responses: Type 1 payoff from bid 0: - Tie when opp bids 0: win prob 1/2, surplus 1-0=1. So \(\pi_1(0) = (1/2) * (x/3) * 1 = x/6.\) Type 1 payoff from bid 1: - Win if opp bids 0: surplus 0? actually v=1, pay 1 → surplus 0. - Tie if opp bids 1: surplus 0. - Lose if opp bids 2. So \(\pi_1(1)=0.\) Thus type 1 prefers bid 0 whenever x>0. In equilibrium, must have x=1 (always bid 0). So type 1 bids 0 purely. Update with x=1: Opponent distribution: P0 = 1/3. P1 = (1/3)*0 + (1/3)y = y/3. P2 = (2 - y)/3. Type 2 payoff from bid 1: - Win if opp bids 0: surplus 2-1=1. - Tie if opp bids 1: win prob 1/2, surplus 1. - Lose if opp bids 2. So: \[ \pi_2(1) = P0*1 + \frac{1}{2}P1*1 = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{y}{3} = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{y}{6}. \] Type 2 payoff from bid 2: - Win if opp bids 0 or 1: surplus 0 (2-2=0). - Tie if opp bids 2: win prob 1/2, surplus 0. So \(\pi_2(2)=0.\) Thus type 2 strictly prefers bid 1. So y=1. Then opponent bids: P0=1/3, P1=1/3, P2=1/3 (since y=1 implies P2=(2-1)/3=1/3). Now type 3 payoff from bid 2: - Win if opp bids 0 or 1: surplus 1 (3-2=1). - Tie if opp bids 2: win prob 1/2, surplus 1. So: \[ \pi_3(2) = (P0+P1)*1 + \frac{1}{2}P2*1 = \left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{5}{6}. \] Type 3 payoff from bid 1: - Win if opp bid 0: surplus 2 (3-1=2). - Tie if opp bid 1: win prob 1/2, surplus 2. - Lose if opp bid 2. So: \[ \pi_3(1) = P0*2 + \frac{1}{2}P1*2 = \frac{1}{3}\cdot 2 + \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{1}{3}\cdot 2 = \frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = 1. \] So type 3 prefers bid 1 to bid 2, contradicting. Then type 2 would want to bid 2 to beat type 3? But type 2 bidding 2 yields 0 surplus, etc. This discrete setup is messy. Conclusion: fully solving a discrete first-price example with mixing that is both clean and not degenerate requires careful choice of bid grid and values; otherwise equilibria collapse to low bids. In practice, mixed equilibria exist but are not as pedagogically clean as continuous models. If your goal is to benchmark strategic reasoning, a better “small complete” solution is a mixed equilibrium in an all-pay auction or a war of attrition. For first-price, continuous types is the standard because it yields closed-form pure strategies.
That’s fair. Let’s not get stuck on discreteness. Switch to asymmetric bidders. How does equilibrium change with different F’s?

With asymmetric distributions \(F_i\), equilibrium typically becomes asymmetric: each bidder i has a bid function \(b_i(v_i)\) solving a system of ODEs. Closed forms are rare. Setup: n bidders, independent private values but not identically distributed. In first-price: If bidder i with value v bids b, probability of winning is \(\prod_{j\ne i} F_j(b_j^{-1}(b))\) assuming strictly increasing \(b_j\). In equilibrium, bidder i chooses b to maximize: \[ (v - b) \prod_{j\ne i} F_j(\beta_j(b)), \] where \(\beta_j = b_j^{-1}\). First-order condition yields differential equations linking \(b_i\) and the inverses of others. Existence/uniqueness can be complex; sometimes equilibrium in monotone strategies exists but may not be unique. Revenue equivalence can fail with asymmetries; expected revenue comparisons across formats become more subtle. Also, allocation may no longer be efficient: the highest valuation might not win if weaker bidders bid more aggressively due to distributional differences (though in first-price with independent private values and monotone strategies, typically allocation is still to highest value if bid functions are increasing in v, but different slopes can cause “bidder advantage” effects). There’s also the “strong bidder” phenomenon: a bidder with stochastically higher values may shade more, potentially allowing weaker bidders to win at times.
Does second-price remain truthful with asymmetries?

Yes. Dominant strategy truthfulness in a single-item second-price auction does not require symmetry or independence; it’s a pointwise argument given others’ bids. So each bidder’s dominant strategy is to bid their true value, regardless of distributions. However, if bidders are asymmetric in values, the expected revenue is still \(E[V_{(n-1)}]\) where \(V_{(n-1)}\) is second-highest among the realized values (from different distributions). That’s well-defined but not as simple as the i.i.d. case.
Now, what about collusion? English auctions are said to be more collusion-prone.

Dynamic auctions can facilitate collusion because bidders can use the bidding path to signal and punish deviations. In an English auction, a cartel might agree that only one member bids actively while others drop out early. If someone deviates and bids against the designated winner, others can retaliate in future auctions (in repeated settings) or even within the same auction by bidding up to punish (though that’s costly). The open format makes monitoring deviations easier. In sealed-bid auctions, it’s harder to detect deviations because bids are private; cartel enforcement is more difficult. This can make sealed bids more resistant to collusion. However, sealed bids can still be colluded via bid rotation or side payments, but enforcement is harder. So the revenue ranking can reverse if collusion is a concern: sealed-bid may yield higher revenue by making collusion less stable.
Can you model collusion stability formally?

A full formal model typically uses repeated games. Simplified repeated-auctions collusion idea: - Auctions occur each period t=1,2,... - Same set of bidders. - Each period, private values drawn i.i.d. - Bidders discount future by δ. A collusive scheme might specify: in each period, the bidder with highest realized value is supposed to win at a low price (e.g., reserve), while others bid low or drop out. But because values are private, incentive constraints are hard: bidders may want to deviate and win even when they’re not designated. In an English auction, as the price ascends, bidders can infer whether the designated winner is indeed high value by their willingness to stay in; cartel can implement “drop out at agreed thresholds” that depend on observed behavior. Deviations are observable (someone staying in too long), enabling trigger strategies: punish by bidding competitively in future periods. This can sustain collusion for sufficiently high δ. In sealed-bid, deviations are not observable, so trigger strategies are less effective; sustaining collusion requires different mechanisms (like bid rotation with external monitoring or communication). Formal incentive constraints depend on the probability of profitable deviation and the punishment severity. The key is observability of actions.
Let’s pivot to mechanism design. You mentioned direct mechanisms. How do auctions fit into revelation principle?

Revelation principle: for many mechanism design problems, you can restrict attention to direct, truthful mechanisms without loss of generality. In auctions: - An indirect mechanism is like a first-price auction: bidders submit bids (actions), which are not necessarily their types. - A direct mechanism asks bidders to report their types (values/signals), and the mechanism assigns allocation and payments based on reports. Revelation principle says: any equilibrium outcome of an indirect mechanism can be replicated by a direct mechanism where truthful reporting is a Bayesian Nash equilibrium. So to characterize implementable outcomes, you can analyze direct mechanisms with incentive compatibility constraints. Myerson uses this to design optimal auctions: choose allocation rule q(v) and payment rule p(v) to maximize expected revenue subject to: - Incentive compatibility: truthful reporting maximizes expected utility. - Individual rationality: participation constraints. Standard auctions are particular indirect mechanisms that implement certain direct mechanisms in equilibrium.
Can you write the incentive compatibility constraint for the single-item case?

In a direct mechanism with allocation probability \(q(v)\) and expected payment \(p(v)\) for a bidder with type v (suppressing others for symmetric case), interim utility: \[ U(v) = v q(v) - p(v). \] Incentive compatibility (IC) requires: \[ v q(v) - p(v) \ge v q(\hat v) - p(\hat v) \quad \forall v,\hat v. \] Under regularity (q nondecreasing), IC is equivalent to the envelope condition: \[ U'(v) = q(v) \] almost everywhere, and \[ U(v) = U(\underline v) + \int_{\underline v}^{v} q(t) dt. \] Then payment is pinned down: \[ p(v) = v q(v) - U(v). \] This is the backbone of revenue equivalence: given q(v) and boundary utility \(U(\underline v)\), payments are determined.
Where does the virtual value come from?

Seller’s expected revenue equals expected payments. Using the envelope representation: \[ E[p(V)] = E[V q(V)] - E[U(V)]. \] With \(U(\underline v)=0\) and \(U(v)=\int_{\underline v}^{v} q(t) dt\), integrate by parts to rewrite expected revenue as: \[ E[p(V)] = E[\phi(V) q(V)] + \text{constant}, \] where \(\phi(v) = v - \frac{1-F(v)}{f(v)}\) is the virtual value. More explicitly, for single bidder: \[ E[p(V)] = \int \left(v - \frac{1-F(v)}{f(v)}\right) q(v) f(v) dv \] assuming boundary terms vanish appropriately. Thus to maximize revenue, pointwise maximize \(\phi(v) q(v)\) subject to feasibility (at most one winner) and monotonicity (IC). In regular cases where \(\phi\) is increasing, allocate to highest φ among bidders, which corresponds to highest v above reserve.
In irregular distributions, what happens?

If \(\phi(v)\) is not monotone increasing (irregular), the naive allocation to highest nonnegative φ may violate IC because it can be nonmonotone in v. Myerson’s solution: “iron” the virtual value—replace φ with its monotone nondecreasing hull (the ironed virtual value). This corresponds to pooling some types: allocation probability becomes flat over regions where φ would decrease. In practice, it can mean randomized allocation among a range of bids or using reserve and bunching. Implementation can be done via optimal auctions that may involve randomization; in many practical settings, sellers approximate with reserves.
How does this relate to business pricing? Is auction just price discrimination?

Auctions can be seen as a form of price discrimination under uncertainty: the seller uses competition and bidding to extract information about willingness to pay and charge a price linked to the second-highest valuation. Compared to posted pricing: - Posted price is like take-it-or-leave-it: seller sets price p; buyer buys if v≥p. - Auction uses competition among multiple buyers to set price endogenously. In Myerson’s framework, optimal auction is akin to optimal price discrimination with multiple buyers; the reserve is analogous to monopoly pricing (based on virtual values). Auctions can yield higher revenue than posted prices when multiple buyers compete, but if buyer arrival is uncertain or bidders have costs, posted prices can be better.
Can you compare expected revenue between optimal reserve auction and no-reserve for Uniform[0,1]?

For n bidders i.i.d. Uniform[0,1]. No reserve second-price revenue: \(E[V_{(n-1)}] = (n-1)/(n+1)\). Optimal reserve r=1/2. In a second-price with reserve: - If highest value < 1/2, no sale, revenue 0. - If highest value ≥ 1/2, winner pays max(reserve, second-highest value). Expected revenue can be computed. Let \(X=V_{(n)}\), \(Y=V_{(n-1)}\). Revenue is: \[ R = \begin{cases} 0 & X < 1/2 \\ 1/2 & X \ge 1/2,\, Y < 1/2 \\ Y & Y \ge 1/2 \end{cases} \] We can compute: \[ E[R] = \frac{1}{2} P(X \ge 1/2, Y < 1/2) + E[Y \mathbf{1}\{Y \ge 1/2\}]. \] Now: - \(P(X < 1/2) = (1/2)^n\). - Event \(X \ge 1/2, Y < 1/2\) means exactly one bidder above 1/2. Probability: \[ n \cdot P(V \ge 1/2) \cdot P(V < 1/2)^{n-1} = n \cdot (1/2)\cdot (1/2)^{n-1} = n/2^n. \] So first term contributes: \[ \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{n}{2^n} = \frac{n}{2^{n+1}}. \] Second term: \(E[Y \mathbf{1}\{Y \ge 1/2\}]\). Density of Y (the (n-1)th order stat) for Uniform: \[ f_Y(y)= \frac{n!}{(n-2)!1!} y^{n-2}(1-y) = n(n-1) y^{n-2}(1-y), \quad y\in[0,1]. \] Then: \[ E[Y \mathbf{1}\{Y \ge 1/2\}] = \int_{1/2}^1 y \cdot n(n-1) y^{n-2}(1-y) dy = n(n-1)\int_{1/2}^1 y^{n-1}(1-y) dy. \] Compute: \[ \int y^{n-1}(1-y) dy = \int (y^{n-1} - y^n) dy = \frac{y^n}{n} - \frac{y^{n+1}}{n+1}. \] Evaluate from 1/2 to 1: At 1: \(1/n - 1/(n+1) = 1/(n(n+1))\). At 1/2: \(( (1/2)^n / n ) - ( (1/2)^{n+1}/(n+1) )\). So integral equals: \[ \frac{1}{n(n+1)} - \frac{1}{2^n}\left(\frac{1}{n} - \frac{1}{2(n+1)}\right) = \frac{1}{n(n+1)} - \frac{1}{2^n}\left(\frac{2(n+1) - n}{2n(n+1)}\right) = \frac{1}{n(n+1)} - \frac{n+2}{2^{n+1} n(n+1)}. \] Multiply by n(n-1): \[ E[Y \mathbf{1}\{Y \ge 1/2\}] = (n-1)\left(\frac{1}{n+1} - \frac{n+2}{2^{n+1}(n+1)}\right) = \frac{n-1}{n+1}\left(1 - \frac{n+2}{2^{n+1}}\right). \] Thus: \[ E[R] = \frac{n}{2^{n+1}} + \frac{n-1}{n+1}\left(1 - \frac{n+2}{2^{n+1}}\right). \] You can compare this to \((n-1)/(n+1)\); the reserve increases revenue (for n≥2) but reduces efficiency because of no-sale states.
That’s a lot of algebra. Good. Now, could you discuss bidder optimal shading in first-price in terms of quantiles?

Yes. Use quantile representation for i.i.d. values. Let \(q = F(v)\) be the quantile, so v = \(F^{-1}(q)\). In symmetric first-price equilibrium (risk-neutral), a known expression is: \[ b(v) = E[V_{(n-1)} \mid V_{(n)} = v] \] Actually in IPV, the equilibrium bid equals expected highest rival value conditional on being the highest? More precisely, in first-price, the expected payment conditional on winning is your bid, while in second-price it’s the highest rival value. In equilibrium, your bid is below v but linked to distribution of rivals. Another quantile formula: \[ b(v) = v - \frac{\int_0^{F(v)} (F^{-1}(t))'??}{(F(v))^{n-1}} \] Better: using envelope expression: \[ (v - b(v))F(v)^{n-1} = \int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt. \] Change variable t = \(F^{-1}(u)\), dt = \(1/f(F^{-1}(u)) du\). Then: \[ \int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt = \int_0^{q} u^{n-1} \frac{1}{f(F^{-1}(u))} du. \] So: \[ b(F^{-1}(q)) = F^{-1}(q) - \frac{\int_0^{q} u^{n-1} \frac{1}{f(F^{-1}(u))} du}{q^{n-1}}. \] In the uniform case, \(f=1\), \(F^{-1}(u)=u\), giving \(b = v - v/n\). Quantile form is useful in empirical auction models: you can estimate F and recover b.
Speaking of empirical models, how do economists estimate valuations from observed bids?

In first-price auctions under IPV, you can invert the equilibrium bidding function to recover valuations from bids, using observed bid distributions. Let G be the CDF of equilibrium bids, with density g. In symmetric equilibrium with n bidders, relationship between v and b is: \[ v = b + \frac{G(b)}{(n-1) g(b)}. \] This comes from rewriting the first-order condition. Derivation: In equilibrium, bidder with value v chooses bid b maximizing \((v-b)G(b)^{n-1}\) if G is bid distribution of a rival (or overall). FOC: \[ 0 = -G(b)^{n-1} + (v-b)(n-1)G(b)^{n-2} g(b). \] Solve for v: \[ v = b + \frac{G(b)}{(n-1) g(b)}. \] This is the Guerre–Perrigne–Vuong (GPV) approach. With data on bids and number of bidders, one estimates G and g nonparametrically and computes pseudo-valuations. For second-price auctions, bids equal values (in IPV), so observed bids are valuations directly (in theory). In practice, reserve prices, bid increments, and strategic behavior can complicate.
In GPV, do you need independence? What if there’s unobserved heterogeneity across auctions?

Standard GPV assumes: - Conditional on observed auction covariates X, bidders’ values are i.i.d. from F(·|X). - Independence across bidders within an auction (conditional on X). - Symmetric equilibrium. Unobserved heterogeneity (auction-level shocks) can induce correlation across bidders’ values within an auction. That biases inversion if ignored. Empirical work addresses this with: - Auction fixed effects if repeated measures exist (rare). - Instrumental variables or control-function approaches. - Modeling heterogeneity explicitly as a multiplicative component (e.g., \(v_{ij} = \eta_j \tilde v_{ij}\)) where \(\eta_j\) is auction-level shock; then use techniques to identify distribution of \(\eta\) and \(\tilde v\). - Using variation in number of bidders and reserve prices. So, yes, independence is important; relaxing it requires more structure.
Back to theory. Can you explain why first-price equilibrium bid is below value? Is it always?

In risk-neutral IPV with at least two bidders and continuous F, yes: equilibrium has shading \(b(v) < v\) for v>0. Reason: In first-price, increasing your bid increases winning probability but also increases payment if you win. If you bid your value, winning yields zero surplus, so you’d like to bid slightly less and still win with high probability. Equilibrium balances this. Formally, from the formula: \[ b(v) = v - \frac{\int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt}{F(v)^{n-1}}. \] The fraction is positive for v>0, so b(v) < v. Also b(0)=0. As v approaches the upper bound, shading typically remains positive but may shrink relative to v depending on distribution and n; as n increases, shading decreases because competition is stronger.
Can we compute shading explicitly for exponential distribution?

Let values be i.i.d. Exponential(λ) on [0,∞): \(F(v)=1-e^{-\lambda v}\), \(f(v)=\lambda e^{-\lambda v}\). Use formula: \[ b(v) = v - \frac{\int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt}{F(v)^{n-1}}. \] The integral doesn’t have a simple elementary form for general n, but can be expressed using series. Expand: \[ F(t)^{n-1} = (1-e^{-\lambda t})^{n-1} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{k} (-1)^k e^{-\lambda k t}. \] Then: \[ \int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{k} (-1)^k \int_0^v e^{-\lambda k t} dt. \] For k=0, integral is v. For k≥1, integral is \(\frac{1 - e^{-\lambda k v}}{\lambda k}\). So: \[ \int_0^v F(t)^{n-1} dt = v + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{k} (-1)^k \frac{1 - e^{-\lambda k v}}{\lambda k}. \] And \(F(v)^{n-1} = (1-e^{-\lambda v})^{n-1}\). Thus: \[ b(v) = v - \frac{v + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{k} (-1)^k \frac{1 - e^{-\lambda k v}}{\lambda k}}{(1-e^{-\lambda v})^{n-1}}. \] Not pretty, but computable. For n=2, it simplifies: n=2: \(F(t)^{1}=1-e^{-\lambda t}\). Integral: \[ \int_0^v (1-e^{-\lambda t}) dt = v - \frac{1-e^{-\lambda v}}{\lambda}. \] Divide by F(v)=1-e^{-\lambda v}: \[ b(v) = v - \frac{v - \frac{1-e^{-\lambda v}}{\lambda}}{1-e^{-\lambda v}} = v - \frac{v}{1-e^{-\lambda v}} + \frac{1}{\lambda}. \] So: \[ b(v) = \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{v e^{-\lambda v}}{1-e^{-\lambda v}}. \] You can check b(v) < v and increasing.
How does the number of bidders affect expected revenue? Is it always increasing?

In standard IPV auctions without reserve, expected revenue increases with n because more competition raises the expected second-highest value. In second-price (and English), expected revenue is \(E[V_{(n-1)}]\), which increases in n for i.i.d. distributions with support bounded below. In first-price, by revenue equivalence under IPV, expected revenue also increases with n. However, with reserves, the effect of n can be nuanced but generally still increasing. With entry costs and endogenous entry, increasing potential bidders can change equilibrium entry in ways that may offset. In common value settings, more bidders can exacerbate winner’s curse, potentially lowering bids and complicating monotonicity; but often more competition still increases expected revenue, though not guaranteed without conditions.
Can you give a condition where more bidders can reduce revenue?

In common value auctions with naive bidders or with certain information structures, adding bidders can increase the severity of winner’s curse: winning becomes worse news, leading to lower equilibrium bids. It’s possible that this effect dominates competition, reducing expected revenue. Formally, in some interdependent value models, expected price can be nonmonotone in n depending on signal precision and affiliation. The general monotonicity results require conditions (like monotone hazard rates, affiliation) and sometimes still yield increasing revenue, but counterexamples exist. Also, with risk-averse seller and risk-neutral bidders? Usually seller revenue increases, but seller’s utility might not. In practice, one also sees “crowding out” from entry costs: if adding more potential bidders lowers each bidder’s expected profit, some may not enter, leading to a similar number of actual bidders.
What about multi-unit auctions, like uniform-price vs discriminatory?

Multi-unit auctions introduce demand reduction and strategic complexity. Two common formats: 1) Discriminatory (pay-as-bid): each winning unit is paid at the bidder’s own bid for that unit. 2) Uniform-price: all winning units pay the same market-clearing price (often the highest losing bid). In uniform-price auctions, bidders may shade bids for marginal units to reduce the clearing price—demand reduction—leading to inefficiency (allocations not to highest values for all units). Discriminatory auctions also have shading but different incentives. Revenue comparisons are ambiguous and depend on demand curves, risk aversion, and correlation. In Treasury auctions, uniform-price was argued to encourage participation and reduce winner’s curse, but discriminatory can yield higher revenue under certain conditions. These are not directly covered by the single-item revenue equivalence theorem.
For single-item, can you explain the role of hazard rate?

Hazard rate \(h(v) = \frac{f(v)}{1-F(v)}\) appears in virtual value and in reserve pricing. Virtual value: \[ \phi(v) = v - \frac{1-F(v)}{f(v)} = v - \frac{1}{h(v)}. \] If hazard rate is increasing (monotone hazard rate, MHR), then φ is increasing (regularity), which simplifies optimal auction: simple reserve works. MHR is also used in comparative statics and approximation guarantees in algorithmic mechanism design (e.g., posted-price approximations). So hazard rate conditions ensure nice monotonicity properties.
Let’s do a quick derivation of Myerson’s reserve for exponential.

Exponential(λ): \(F(v)=1-e^{-\lambda v}\), \(f(v)=\lambda e^{-\lambda v}\). Then \((1-F)/f = e^{-\lambda v}/(\lambda e^{-\lambda v}) = 1/\lambda\). So virtual value: \[ \phi(v)= v - \frac{1}{\lambda}. \] Set φ(r)=0 → \(r=1/\lambda\). So optimal reserve equals the monopoly price for exponential distribution.
Good. Now, can you address a subtle point: in English auctions with interdependent values, what is the equilibrium strategy?

In interdependent values, equilibrium strategies in English auctions are typically characterized by “dropout prices” as functions of your signal and the information revealed so far. In a symmetric model where each bidder’s value is \(v(s_i, s_{-i})\) and signals are affiliated, a common equilibrium concept is that each bidder stays in until the price equals their expected value conditional on their signal and the event that remaining bidders’ signals exceed the dropout signals observed so far. At any point, suppose k bidders remain and you have observed dropout prices corresponding to some inferred signals for those who left. Your posterior about V (or about others’ signals) updates. Your continuation value is: \[ E[v_i \mid s_i, \text{information from dropouts}, \text{others still in}], \] and you drop out when price reaches that conditional expected value. This is more complex than the private-values case because your value depends on others’ signals, and the history matters. But under affiliation and monotonicity, there exists a symmetric equilibrium in monotone “cutoff” strategies. In pure common value with signals, the equilibrium dropout price can be characterized as the expected value conditional on being the highest among remaining signals and given observed dropouts.
Is there a closed-form for the common value English auction?

In general, no. Closed forms exist in special cases (e.g., certain distributions, linear-normal models) but often require numerical methods. In the classic “mineral rights” model with signals and common value, equilibrium bidding in English auctions can sometimes be expressed as: \[ p(s) = E[V \mid s, S_{(n-1)}=s] \] or related conditional expectations, depending on model and how dropout reveals information. In linear-normal (Gaussian) settings: - \(V\sim N(\mu_0,\sigma_0^2)\). - Signals \(s_i = V + \epsilon_i\), \(\epsilon_i\sim N(0,\sigma_\epsilon^2)\). Then posteriors are normal, and conditional expectations are linear in signals. The difficulty is conditioning on order statistics (being highest) and on observed dropouts; still tractable with some work. But the core qualitative result—English yields higher expected revenue than sealed-bid due to information revelation—doesn’t require closed forms.
Ok. Another topic: what’s the difference between first-price and second-price in terms of robustness to bidder mistakes?

Second-price is theoretically “simple” (truthful), but can be sensitive to mistakes like overbidding (bidding above value) which can cause negative payoffs. In first-price, overbidding is also harmful, but shading behavior may reduce the likelihood of catastrophic overbids if bidders are cautious. However, in experimental economics, people often overbid in second-price auctions relative to dominant strategy, especially with private values, which can inflate revenue but is not equilibrium behavior. In first-price, bidders also deviate, often bidding above risk-neutral equilibrium (consistent with risk aversion or bounded rationality). Robustness depends on what mistakes are likely: - If bidders may not understand dominance, second-price can produce erratic outcomes. - If bidders have biased beliefs about others, first-price can be more sensitive. From a mechanism design perspective, dominant-strategy mechanisms are robust to belief misspecification, but not necessarily to behavioral deviations.
Let’s talk about equilibrium selection in first-price. Is equilibrium unique?

In the standard IPV model with continuous F, risk-neutral bidders, and strictly increasing strategies, the symmetric equilibrium bid function is unique within the class of increasing differentiable strategies satisfying boundary condition b(0)=0. That comes from the ODE with boundary condition, which has a unique solution. There can be other equilibria if you allow nonmonotone strategies or mixed strategies in pathological cases, but the standard equilibrium is essentially unique and focal. In asymmetric models, multiple equilibria can exist. In discrete models, multiple equilibria and mixing are common.
What about the seller’s optimal mechanism when bidders are risk-averse?

With risk-averse bidders, the seller can exploit risk preferences by using mechanisms that impose more payment risk on bidders (who dislike it) to extract more revenue? Actually, if bidders are risk-averse, they prefer less variance in payments. The seller (assumed risk-neutral) can design mechanisms that shift risk onto bidders in exchange for higher expected payments. Classic result: With risk-averse bidders, the optimal auction may resemble a first-price auction (or an all-pay) rather than a second-price, because first-price makes payments more deterministic conditional on winning (you know what you pay), whereas second-price exposes you to uncertainty about the price (depends on others). Risk-averse bidders may bid more aggressively in first-price, raising revenue. More formally, the revenue equivalence theorem fails, and optimal mechanism depends on utility curvature. The seller may choose a mechanism that increases the correlation between winning and payment certainty. But the full optimal design with risk-averse bidders can be complex; it’s not just “pick first-price.”
That seems counterintuitive: isn’t second-price less risky because you pay someone else’s bid?

From the bidder’s perspective: - In first-price, if you win you pay exactly your bid—deterministic conditional on your action. - In second-price, if you win you pay the highest rival bid—random conditional on winning. Risk-averse bidders dislike that conditional price risk. They may respond by shading differently (though truthful bidding remains dominant in private values, the dominance is about expected utility pointwise given others’ bids; but the uncertainty in payment still exists across states where you win). Even though you can’t change the distribution of the price conditional on winning by your bid (beyond affecting whether you win), the mechanism exposes you to random payments whenever you do win. A risk-averse bidder might prefer a mechanism where winning payoff is more predictable, but in second-price they can’t insure away price variability. In first-price, by choosing your bid you choose the price you pay, reducing risk. That can make first-price more attractive to risk-averse bidders, leading to higher bids and higher seller revenue compared to second-price, holding valuations fixed.
But if truthful bidding is dominant in second-price, how can risk aversion change revenue comparisons? Bids don’t change.

Exactly: in second-price IPV, bids remain equal to values regardless of risk aversion. Therefore the distribution of payments (second-highest value) is unchanged. In first-price, bids do change with risk aversion (less shading), increasing payments. Hence first-price revenue rises relative to second-price. That’s the channel.
Now, can you discuss optimal information disclosure by the seller?

Seller can choose what information about the object to reveal before the auction (e.g., inspection reports, quality signals). In interdependent/common value settings, disclosure affects bidder estimates and winner’s curse. General insight: - More public information can increase expected revenue if it reduces uncertainty and increases bidders’ willingness to bid (reduces winner’s curse). - But information can also reduce revenue if it dampens competition by making bidders’ valuations more aligned or by reducing dispersion (in some models, more information can reduce expected second-highest value). With affiliated signals and certain conditions, revealing more information tends to increase revenue in English auctions (linkage principle). But optimal disclosure is nuanced; sometimes partial disclosure is optimal. In private values, seller information about the object can shift the valuation distribution; whether it increases revenue depends on how it affects the distribution of order statistics.
Can you relate that to signaling and persuasion?

It connects to Bayesian persuasion: the seller designs an information structure (signal) to maximize expected revenue given bidders’ responses. In auctions, persuasion interacts with strategic bidding. The seller chooses what to reveal about the item (state) to influence the distribution of bidders’ posterior valuations. This is an active research area: optimal signaling in auctions, combining mechanism design and information design. Results depend on whether the seller can commit, whether bidders have private information, and auction format.
What about reserve price vs disclosure tradeoff?

Reserves screen out low valuations and extract more from high types. Disclosure can shift valuations upward by reducing uncertainty or by changing beliefs. They can be substitutes or complements: - If better disclosure increases bidders’ expected values and reduces winner’s curse, seller might need a higher reserve to capitalize. - If disclosure increases competition and expected prices, reserve might be less crucial. In Myerson’s IPV world, reserves are optimal; disclosure is irrelevant unless it changes F. In common value world, disclosure is central.
Let’s do another formal comparison: expected revenue in English vs first-price under common values.

In general common value settings, there isn’t a universal ranking without conditions. Under affiliated signals and certain regularity, the linkage principle suggests English (and second-price) yield weakly higher expected revenue than first-price. A formal comparison often proceeds by: - Characterizing equilibrium bidding functions in each format as conditional expectations. - Showing that the expected payment in English is increasing in information revealed, and English reveals more information than first-price. - Using affiliation to show monotone shifts in conditional expectations. But explicit formulas depend on the joint distribution. A stylized statement: in a symmetric common value model with affiliated signals, the expected price in an English auction equals the expected value conditional on the second-highest signal (because the second-highest bidder is pivotal in dropout), whereas in first-price the winner conditions only on having the highest signal, which is “worse news” and leads to more shading. Thus English yields higher expected price.
I’m thinking about “does the highest value always win?” In common values, there isn’t a highest value since values are same. So what does efficiency mean?

Good point. In pure common value, ex post value is the same for all bidders (V), so allocation doesn’t matter for efficiency—any winner yields the same realized value. Efficiency concerns are trivial. In interdependent values (not pure common), different bidders may have different private components, and ex post values can differ. Then efficiency means allocating to bidder with highest realized value \(v_i(s)\) (given all signals), which may not coincide with highest signal. Often mechanisms allocate to highest signal because that’s implementable and aligns with higher expected value, but it may not be ex post efficient. So in common value, the main concern is revenue and winner’s curse, not allocative efficiency.
Can you define winner’s curse precisely?

Winner’s curse is the phenomenon that the expected value of the object conditional on winning is lower than the bidder’s unconditional estimate based on their own information. Formally, if bidder i’s estimate based on signal s is \(E[V \mid s_i=s]\), then: \[ E[V \mid s_i=s, \text{i wins}] < E[V \mid s_i=s] \] in common value settings with noisy signals, because winning is correlated with having an unusually high signal realization (positive noise). If bidders ignore this conditioning, they overbid and may earn negative expected profits. In equilibrium with rational bidders, they account for it by shading bids to reflect \(E[V \mid s_i=s, \text{win}]\).
Another question: what’s the “revenue ranking” between first-price and second-price when bidders have budgets?

Budgets can reverse rankings. If bidders have hard budget constraints B, they cannot bid above B and cannot pay above B. In second-price, a bidder might want to bid their value, but if value exceeds budget, they bid at most B. This can distort allocation and pricing. In first-price, bidders already shade; budgets can bind differently, potentially leading to higher or lower revenue. Sometimes second-price with budgets yields lower revenue because high-valuation bidders are capped, reducing competition for price. First-price can sometimes extract more from budget-constrained bidders because they pay their bid and may bid up to budget to win, but it depends. There’s no general ranking; you need the distribution of values and budgets. Budget constraints also complicate incentive properties and may require mechanisms like pacing (common in ad auctions).
Let’s do a quick example with budgets to see non-equivalence.

Consider two bidders, private values v1, v2, but both have budget B=1. Values can be >1. Suppose v’s are i.i.d. Uniform[0,2]. Risk-neutral. Second-price with budget cap: Each bids \(b_i = \min(v_i, 1)\). Winner pays min(second-highest bid, budget) = second-highest bid (already ≤1). So price is at most 1. First-price with budget cap: Bids also cannot exceed 1. But equilibrium shading with truncated types differs: a bidder with v>1 behaves like a type with value effectively 1? Actually their marginal value of winning is still v, but they can’t pay more than 1, so they may bid 1 aggressively. Many high-value types bunch at bid 1, increasing tie probability and changing expected revenue. Thus revenue equivalence breaks: second-price price is second-highest truncated value; first-price may have more mass at 1 and may yield higher revenue due to more frequent bids at the cap. But you’d need to solve equilibrium with caps to quantify. Main point: budgets create bunching and non-smooth strategies; standard theorems don’t apply.
Now, can you explain the concept of “optimal bidding” in a first-price auction from a single bidder’s perspective, given others’ bid distribution?

If you’re a bidder with value v and you believe the highest rival bid has CDF H(b), then if you bid b, you win with probability H(b). In first-price, expected payoff: \[ \pi(b) = (v-b) H(b). \] Optimal b solves FOC (assuming interior): \[ \pi'(b) = -H(b) + (v-b) h(b) = 0 \] where h is density. So: \[ v - b = \frac{H(b)}{h(b)}. \] Thus optimal bid satisfies: \[ b = v - \frac{H(b)}{h(b)}. \] In symmetric equilibrium, H is derived from the distribution of others’ values and their bidding strategy, leading to the inversion formula earlier. This expression shows the tradeoff: shading equals inverse hazard rate of the rival-bid distribution.
That looks like monopoly pricing math.

Exactly. The term \(H/h\) is the inverse hazard rate of the competitor bid distribution. This is analogous to monopoly markup \( (1-F)/f\) in pricing. In both cases, optimality equates marginal benefit and marginal cost, and the inverse hazard rate captures how quickly winning probability increases with bid. This analogy is used in algorithmic bidding and auction design: bid shading resembles setting a price against a demand curve.
Let’s connect that to business strategy: if a firm participates in procurement auctions, how should it think about shading?

In procurement (reverse auctions), lowest bid wins; bidder’s “value” is cost c. The analog of first-price is pay-your-bid but you receive your bid as payment; profit is (bid - cost) if you win. Optimal shading now is markup above cost, balancing probability of winning against margin. If you model competitors’ lowest bid distribution, you choose bid b to maximize: \[ (b - c) \cdot P(\text{win with } b) = (b-c) \cdot P(\text{others bid } > b). \] That’s again a markup formula involving hazard rates of rivals’ bids/costs. Learning the distribution from historical tenders is key. Risk preferences, capacity constraints, and dynamic considerations (reputation) matter.
In procurement, is the “second-price” format used?

Less often, but variants exist (Vickrey procurement auctions) where the lowest bidder wins but is paid the second-lowest bid. In theory, that induces truthful reporting of costs (bid your cost). But in practice, it’s rare due to concerns about overpayment, collusion, and political acceptability (“paying more than the winning bid” looks suspicious). Also, cost verification and renegotiation risks complicate. So pay-as-bid (first-price) is more common in procurement.
Great. Now, can you summarize the main mathematical differences among the three formats I asked about, in one coherent structure?
